questions.question_text: A licensee struggling with alcohol dependency wants to seek help without losing her license. What Delaware provision addresses this?
distractors: The Guaranty Fund reimbursement process
Mandatory substance abuse reporting rules
The Commission's automatic suspension policy
questions.explanation: Section 15.0 of the Commission's Rules (24 DE Admin. Code 2900) provides a Voluntary Treatment Option for chemically dependent or impaired professionals. A licensee who enters an approved treatment agreement may continue practicing, subject to any necessary practice limitations, and the matter is handled non-disciplinarily as long as she cooperates and progresses satisfactorily. (The option was numbered Rule 14 before the Commission's 2020 renumbering, and older materials may still use that label.)

questions.question_text: What types of conditions does the Voluntary Treatment Option address?
questions.correct_answer: Chemical dependency or impairment from drugs or alcohol
distractors: Substance abuse and all mental health conditions
Only alcohol-related impairment
Physical disabilities affecting job duties
questions.explanation: The Voluntary Treatment Option applies to regulated professionals who are chemically dependent or impaired due to addiction to drugs or alcohol (24 DE Admin. Code 2900-15.4). It is not limited to alcohol, but it also does not extend to mental-health or physical conditions unrelated to drug or alcohol addiction.

questions.question_text: A licensee enters the Voluntary Treatment Option while facing a Commission complaint. How might participation affect the disciplinary outcome?
questions.correct_answer: It may result in more favorable outcomes
distractors: It guarantees full dismissal of charges
It has no effect on disciplinary proceedings
It automatically suspends the investigation
questions.explanation: A licensee who enters the Voluntary Treatment Option, cooperates, and completes the program has the matter handled non-disciplinarily — participation records do not reflect disciplinary action and are not public (24 DE Admin. Code 2900-15.6.7). Completion is not a guaranteed dismissal: failure to comply or progress triggers immediate investigation and disciplinary proceedings (2900-15.6.10).

questions.question_text: A colleague asks the Commission whether a licensee is enrolled in the Voluntary Treatment Option (Rule 15). How should the Commission respond?
questions.correct_answer: Decline to disclose due to confidentiality
distractors: Confirm enrollment as public information
Disclose only to the licensee's broker
Share details if the colleague is licensed
questions.explanation: Voluntary Treatment Option participation is confidential — records of participation do not reflect disciplinary action and are not public records (24 DE Admin. Code 2900-15.6.7, 15.6.12). The Commission will not disclose whether a licensee is enrolled, regardless of who asks. This confidentiality encourages licensees to seek help without fear of professional stigma or reputational harm.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the relationship between the Voluntary Treatment Option and formal discipline?
questions.correct_answer: It can serve as an alternative or supplement
distractors: It completely replaces formal discipline
It applies only after license revocation
It is limited to first-time offenders
questions.explanation: The Voluntary Treatment Option can function as an alternative to disciplinary action or run alongside it — a compliant participant's matter is handled non-disciplinarily, while noncompliance triggers immediate investigation and discipline (24 DE Admin. Code 2900-15.5, 15.6.10). There is no restriction limiting the option to first offenses, though prior participation records may be considered if dependency is at issue in a future disciplinary matter (2900-15.6.7).

questions.question_text: Which Delaware statute prohibits deceptive practices in real estate transactions?
questions.correct_answer: The Consumer Fraud Act under 6 Del C Ch 25
distractors: Title 24 Chapter 29 licensing statute only
The Delaware Uniform Commercial Code
The federal Real Estate Settlement Act
questions.explanation: The Consumer Fraud Act (6 Del C Ch 25) broadly prohibits deceptive trade practices, including those in real estate. This is separate from the Commission's licensing statute and gives consumers an additional avenue for relief through the Attorney General's office.

questions.question_text: Who enforces the Delaware Consumer Fraud Act against deceptive real estate practices?
distractors: The Real Estate Commission only
The Division of Professional Regulation
The Delaware Department of Housing
questions.explanation: The Attorney General enforces the Consumer Fraud Act. While the Commission handles licensing violations, the AG has separate authority to pursue deceptive trade practices. This creates two enforcement tracks that can operate simultaneously against a licensee.

questions.question_text: A licensee engages in deceptive advertising that violates both licensing law and consumer fraud law. What penalties could she face?
questions.correct_answer: Both Commission discipline and consumer fraud penalties
distractors: Only Commission discipline, not civil penalties
Only consumer fraud penalties from the AG
She must choose which forum to face charges
questions.explanation: Delaware licensees can face dual consequences: Commission disciplinary action under the licensing statute and consumer fraud penalties under 6 Del C Ch 25. These are separate proceedings, and action in one does not preclude the other. The Attorney General may seek injunctions, restitution, and civil penalties up to $10,000 per willful violation (6 Del. C. §2522), and private victims have their own cause of action under 6 Del. C. §2525.

questions.question_text: A consumer wins a Consumer Fraud Act case against a licensee. What remedies are available?
questions.correct_answer: Compensatory damages, plus punitive damages in egregious cases
distractors: Damages plus attorney fees for every prevailing consumer
Criminal penalties and imprisonment
Treble damages for all consumers regardless of age
questions.explanation: A private Consumer Fraud Act victim may recover compensatory damages, and Delaware courts permit punitive damages where the fraud is egregious. The statute contains no general fee-shifting; treble damages, court costs, and reasonable attorney's fees are available only when the victim is an elder person (65+) or a person with a disability (6 Del. C. §2583).

questions.question_text: A real estate agent recommends an unlicensed friend to perform a home inspection. What does Delaware law require of home inspectors?
questions.correct_answer: Home inspectors must hold a state license
distractors: No license is required for home inspectors
Only a general contractor license is needed
Certification from a national body suffices
questions.explanation: Delaware requires home inspectors to be state-licensed. Recommending an unlicensed inspector could expose the agent to liability and potential discipline. Licensees should verify that any home inspector they recommend holds a current Delaware license.
